paper_title: Multiscale Material Modeling of Fired Clay Bricks: Experimental Characterization of Microstructural Morphometry and Continuum Mechanical Prediction of Thermal Conductivity, Elastic Stiffness, and Strength Properties
source_file: 机理文献/核心文献/15_Multiscale material modeling of fired clay bricks Experimental characterization of microstructural.pdf
author: Thomas Buchner

render_review.all_pages: Rendered to PNG at 110 dpi and visually checked in nine 16-page contact sheets.
render_review.notes: Retained equations, parameter tables, captions, axes, units and all three extracted figure crops were rechecked against high-resolution page renders.

sections_checked: Chapter 2: A multitechnique quantitative characterization of the pore space, Sections 2.1-2.6
Chapter 3: Automated morphometrical characterization of material phases, Sections 3.1-3.5 and Appendix A
Chapter 4: Continuum micromechanics model for elastic stiffness and thermal conductivity, Sections 4.1-4.4 and Appendix A
Chapter 5: Validated multiscale model for multiaxial strength, Sections 5.1-5.5
Chapter 6: Summary, conclusions and outlook, Sections 6.1-6.4
Bibliography, curriculum vitae and dissemination pages for document completeness only

equations_checked.notes: Porosity, true-density, micro-CT intravoxel and 2D/3D pore-morphometry relations were inspected. They support measurement provenance but are not needed in the minimal contact-failure card.
Phase chemistry, mass-to-volume conversion and ellipse-to-spheroid morphometry relations were inspected; retained outputs are represented by Table 3.5 and the phase-parameter ranges.
Retained as E1. The cosh numerator, sinh denominator, kappa parameter and theta-domain/azimuth assumptions were visually checked.
Retained as E2. Phase fraction, double orientation integral, stiffness-strain-concentration contraction and sin(theta)/(4 pi) measure were visually checked.
Visually checked as the phase strain-concentration tensor used by E2/E5; omitted as a separate full formula to keep the evidence card compact.
Scale-specific fractions, transversely isotropic phase compliance and shear-modulus approximation were checked. Numerical phase inputs are retained in the parameter table.
Binding-matrix Ez = 32.79 + 1.18 m_CaO+MgO GPa was verified but omitted because it is a two-material linear fit and is unsafe for direct transfer.
Ultrasound stiffness and wavelength scale-separation checks were inspected; exact validated stiffness ranges and errors are retained instead.
Micro/meso/macroscale size hierarchy was visually checked and retained as explicit parameter rows and applicability conditions.

Retained as E3. Maximum and minimum principal stresses, both trigonometric factors, denominator 2 c cos(phi), final minus one and f_MC=0 condition were visually checked. Compression is negative according to Section 5.3.3.
Retained as E4; binding-matrix spatial-average stress equals the fourth-order stress concentration tensor contracted with macrostress.
Retained as E5; the ordered contractions C_bm:A_bm:A_fm:(C_brick)^-1 were visually checked.
Verified as the Chapter 5 restatement of the two-scale Mori-Tanaka homogenization and strain-concentration equations. Eq. (5.5) is represented by E2; Eq. (5.6) is referenced but not repeated.
Transversely isotropic phase-compliance matrix inspected; key directional moduli and assumptions are retained in the parameter table.

tables_checked.notes: True-density comparison and method-scope/bias table inspected. Only measurement limitations needed for V1 are retained.
Volume and mass fractions of matrix, pores and mineral phases for bricks A-E checked; condensed ranges are retained instead of all specimens.
Aspect ratios, measured orientation scatter and ODF kappa values checked. The apparent degree sign on one kappa entry is a table typo/formatting artifact and that entry is not reproduced.
Phase-specific directional moduli, Poisson ratios and conductivity values checked. Relevant stiffness values and their provenance are retained.
Brick densities, ultrasound stiffness components and thermal conductivity values checked. Only stiffness ranges relevant to local material response are retained.
Reference-brick phase fractions, aspect ratios, ODF parameters and directional elastic constants checked; condensed phase fraction vector and key moduli are retained.
Equivalent isotropic x- and z-direction FE bounds for binding, foam and brick matrices checked; not reproduced because they are validation-model surrogates rather than material constants.
Triaxial loading paths A/B and failure stresses checked. The normalized validation result is retained in V4 and Fig. 5.12.

figures_checked.reason: Porosity gradients, method comparison and pore-size distributions support measurement-method limitations that are compactly preserved in V1.
Its multiscale geometry is superseded by Fig. 5.1, which also locates the binding-matrix failure criterion.
Stiffness validation is more compactly and precisely represented by reported component-wise mean prediction errors.
Large-quartz intact/cracked bounds and the 10 micrometer threshold are preserved textually with their empirical limitation.
Irreplaceable joint definition of micro/meso/macroscales, phase hierarchy, spheroid axes, coordinates and orientation distribution; crop includes all labels and legend.
Macroscopic test dimensions and nanoindentation FE geometry are adequately represented by the parameter table and scale warnings.
Minimal direct microscale validation of both direction-dependent unloading curves against the FE bounds; crop preserves axes, units, legend and all panels.
Sensitivity to cohesion and friction angle is stated qualitatively because the plot does not provide an independently reusable calibration dataset.
Failure-surface shape and directional tensile/compressive/shear strengths are retained numerically; these plots are model outputs rather than independent validation.
Minimal direct macroscale comparison of two normalized triaxial loading paths with predicted meridians; crop preserves axes, units, legend and data points.
Biaxial-to-uniaxial ratios are more precisely retained as 0.80, 1.01, 1.14 versus the modeled 0.88.

omitted_sections: General brick history, construction motivation and literature-history narrative
Detailed sample preparation, instrument operation and chemical/mineralogical tables not affecting the retained mechanical mechanisms
Thermal-conductivity homogenization, heat-flow testing and cross-property analysis except where needed to establish model scope
Appendix phase-image plates after checking their layout and phase-label consistency
Acknowledgments, full bibliography, curriculum vitae and scientific dissemination lists

uncertain_items.detail: The doctoral thesis itself does not report a DOI. Chapter 5 is a published article with DOI 10.1016/j.mechmat.2022.104334, which is recorded separately and not attributed to the entire thesis.
One muscovite kappa entry is rendered with a degree sign although kappa is dimensionless. That individual entry is not reproduced.

The largest mesophase size is at most 150 micrometers while the mesoscopic RVE is 300 micrometers; the paper explicitly accepts factors of only 2-3 for its larger-than relation. Applicability is weaker for a sharp contact zone.
Cohesion and friction angle are back-identified from nominal macroscopic bending/compression strengths, not directly measured on the binding matrix.

Published test bricks lack microstructural inputs. Test and model stresses are normalized by their own uniaxial compressive strengths, validating failure-surface shape but not absolute cross-brick strength transfer.
The model uses spatial-average binding-matrix stress in an RVE. The thesis does not define how a micrometer-scale spine contact field should be averaged or how local pore-edge peaks alter initiation.

known_warnings: Internal pore/mineral morphometry is not external rough-surface topography and is routed only to the material-failure part of M2.
Strength modeling is calibrated to one calcareous brick fired at 880 C; seven-brick coverage applies to stiffness, not to identified failure constants.
The 10 micrometer large-quartz zero-stiffness rule is an empirical hard threshold rather than an interface-debonding law.
Ordinary Mori-Tanaka homogenization yields a non-symmetric tensor for anisotropic non-spherical phases; the thesis applies post-symmetrization as an approximation.
The failure model predicts first attainment only and excludes post-peak softening, fracture energy, crack path, crushing, scratching, cyclic damage and rate effects.
Macroscopic stiffness and strength values must not be used directly as micrometer-scale spine-contact parameters without a local-to-RVE bridge and target-brick calibration.
character_count_method: Count of CJK Unified Ideographs in evidence_card.md; within the allowed 3500-character extension because the thesis contains separate morphology, elastic-homogenization and multiaxial-strength submodels.
